title: Evolution of NVIDIA Data Center GPUs: From Pascal to Grace Blackwell
text:

Evolution of NVIDIA Data Center GPUs: From Pascal to Grace Blackwell (2016–2025)
Evolution of NVIDIA Data Center GPUs: From Pascal to Grace Blackwell (2016–2025)
NVIDIA’s data-center accelerators have advanced dramatically over the past decade, culminating in the 2025 Grace Blackwell GB200 generation. This analysis walks through each major GPU generation – Pascal, Volta, Ampere, Hopper, the Hopper-based H200/Grace Hopper, and finally Blackwell – highlighting architectural improvements, performance gains, memory enhancements, AI capabilities, and power efficiency at each step. Comparative tables are included to summarize key specifications across generations.
Check out our latest Custom GPU Servers for all needs.
NVIDIA Pascal (TeslaNVIDIA Pascal (Tesla P100, 2016) – Baseline HPC GPU with HBM2 and NVLink P100, 2016) – Baseline HPC GPU with HBM2 and NVLink
Pascal marked NVIDIA’s first GPU architecture purpose-built for both HPC and emerging AI workloads in the data center. The Tesla P100 (GP100 GPU) introduced several foundational technologies that set the stage for later accelerators:
- New Process & Transistor Count: Built on TSMC’s 16nm FinFET, the GP100 packed 15.3 billion transistors – roughly double its Maxwell predecessor – enabling higher compute density.
- High-Bandwidth Memory: P100 was the first GPU to use HBM2 memory (16 GB on-package), delivering 720 GB/s bandwidth – a huge jump over GDDR5. This allowed feeding data-hungry HPC kernels more efficiently.
- NVLink Interconnect: Debuting NVLink v1, P100 could connect GPUs with up to 160 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth (5x PCIe3) for efficient multi-GPU scaling. This peer-to-peer link enabled faster communication in multi-GPU servers and NVLink “Hybrid Cube Mesh” topologies.
- Compute Performance: Pascal retained a strong focus on FP64 for HPC (5.3 TFLOPS FP64) while boosting FP32 to 10.6 TFLOPS. It also introduced enhanced FP16 support (21.2 TFLOPS half-precision), foreshadowing the importance of lower precision in AI workloads. No dedicated tensor cores existed yet, but P100’s ability to operate on half-precision at twice the rate of FP32 was an early nod to deep learning acceleration.
- Power Efficiency: At ~300W TDP, P100 delivered big performance leaps without increasing power over prior-gen (Maxwell) accelerators. The move to 16nm and architectural optimizations yielded better performance per watt, critical for scaling GPU compute in data centers.
Summary: Pascal P100 established the baseline for modern HPC GPUs with high-bandwidth HBM2 memory and NVLink connectivity. It significantly improved raw compute throughput and memory bandwidth, setting the stage for specialized AI hardware in the next generation.
NVIDIA Volta (Tesla V100, 2017) – Tensor Cores Debut for AI
Volta introduced profound architectural innovations, most notably Tensor Cores, transforming GPUs into AI powerhouses. The Tesla V100 (GV100 GPU) was NVIDIA’s first GPU to incorporate dedicated matrix-multiply units and other enhancements targeted at deep learning, while still pushing HPC performance:
- Tensor Cores for AI: V100 featured 640 Tensor Cores (8 per SM) that delivered up to 125 TFLOPS of FP16 deep learning performance – a 12× increase in tensor throughput over Pascal. These specialized units perform $4\times4$ matrix multiply–accumulate operations in mixed precision, enabling massive speedups for neural network training and inference. Volta’s introduction of tensor core acceleration was a turning point for AI computing.
- High FP64 and New CUDA Cores: With 5,120 FP32 CUDA cores (80 SMs), V100 reached ~15.7 TFLOPS FP32 and 7.8 TFLOPS FP64 (double-precision). It maintained a 1:2 ratio of FP64:FP32 throughput, crucial for traditional HPC workloads. Volta also improved scheduling (independent thread scheduling) to better handle divergent code, improving GPU utilization in complex HPC and AI tasks.
- Memory Upgrades: Like P100, V100 used HBM2 but offered configurations up to 16 GB (initially) and later 32 GB, doubling on-board memory for large datasets. Memory bandwidth increased to 900 GB/s on the SXM2 module, and architecture refinements (larger caches and on-chip memory) kept data feeds steady to the compute units.
- NVLink 2.0: NVIDIA doubled down on multi-GPU connectivity – Volta’s NVLink v2 featured 6 links per GPU at ~25 GB/s each, for an aggregate 300 GB/s GPU–GPU bandwidth (up from Pascal’s 160 GB/s). This allowed more GPUs to be efficiently clustered (e.g., 8x V100 in a DGX-1 server) with fast communication through NVLink switches.
- Power and Efficiency: Fabricated on a custom 12nm TSMC process, V100 grew to 21.1 billion transistors on a large 815 mm² die. TDP remained ~300W (SXM2), similar to P100, but performance per watt improved substantially thanks to the massive increase in throughput. NVIDIA noted up to 12× higher deep learning performance per GPU with Volta, translating to big efficiency gains for AI workloads even at comparable power.
Summary: Volta V100 was a landmark, introducing tensor core architecture that yielded an order-of-magnitude leap in AI performance. It balanced this with strong FP64 capability for HPC, making it a versatile engine for both scientific computing and deep learning. NVLink and memory improvements further ensured V100 could tackle ever-larger models and datasets with better efficiency than its predecessor.
NVIDIA Ampere (A100, 2020) – Expanded AI Throughput and Scalability
Ampere’s A100 built on Volta’s foundation with an aggressive node shrink and many architectural enhancements that boosted both general-purpose and AI-specific performance. It became the workhorse of the AI boom in the early 2020s (notably powering systems like OpenAI’s GPT-3 training). Key advancements of the A100 include:
- 7nm Process & Massive Transistor Count: The GA100 GPU in A100 jumped to 54.2 billion transistors on TSMC’s 7nm process – over 2.5× the transistor count of V100. This enabled 108 SMs (Streaming Multiprocessors) with 6,912 FP32 cores in total, significantly increasing raw throughput. Ampere doubled the FP32 ALUs per SM, so A100 delivers 19.5 TFLOPS FP32 and 9.7 TFLOPS FP64 (FP64 at 1/2 rate) despite a modest clock speed (~1.4 GHz). The combination of more cores and 7nm efficiency improved A100’s performance/Watt by roughly 20% over V100.
- Third-Gen Tensor Cores (TF32 & BF16): Ampere introduced third-generation Tensor Cores that added support for new math formats. Notably, TensorFloat-32 (TF32) computation allows treating FP32 inputs with lower precision internally, yielding up to 156 TFLOPS of tensor performance (312 TFLOPS with structured sparsity) – effectively accelerating FP32 matrix ops by ~8× compared to V100. A100’s tensor cores also support BFloat16 (a format popular in AI) and FP64 tensor operations, providing up to 19.5 TFLOPS of double-precision via tensor cores for HPC workloads. NVIDIA reported these enhanced tensor cores can provide up to 20× speedups vs. Volta on AI training tasks by leveraging TF32 and sparsity.
- Structural Sparsity: A100 introduced hardware acceleration for sparse matrices, automatically skipping multiply-adds for zeros. In supported layers, two out of four weights can be zeroed, doubling throughput with minimal impact on accuracy. This yields up to 2× performance gain on inference of pruned neural networks, effectively boosting A100’s 312 TFLOPS FP16 to 624 TFLOPS on sparse workloads.
- Multi-Instance GPU (MIG): A100 can be partitioned into up to 7 separate GPU instances, each with dedicated SMs, memory, and cache resources. MIG enables one A100 to serve multiple jobs or users at once, improving utilization and QoS in cloud environments. For example, one A100 can run seven independent 5-GB GPU tasks simultaneously – a big efficiency gain for lighter inference workloads or multi-tenant uses.
- Memory and Bandwidth: Early A100 cards shipped with 40 GB HBM2 (1.6 TB/s bandwidth), later doubled to 80 GB HBM2e running at 3.2 Gbps (bringing memory bandwidth to 2.0 TB/s). The 2.5× memory size increase over V100 (32→80 GB) allowed A100 to handle larger models entirely on-GPU, reducing CPU offload. L2 cache was also enlarged (40 MB) to feed the beast of 54B transistors with data.
- NVLink 3 and Interconnects: Ampere’s third-gen NVLink increased GPU–GPU bandwidth to 600 GB/s (aggregate) per card by using 12 links at 50 GB/s each. This high-speed interconnect, along with support for PCIe 4.0, improved multi-GPU scaling and connectivity to fast storage/network. An 8×A100 HGX board uses NVSwitch to fully connect all GPUs at full NVLink speed, enabling big model training across GPUs with minimal communication bottlenecks.
- Power: A100 SXM modules had a 400W TDP. Despite higher power than V100 (300W), the performance gains were greater – yielding better overall efficiency. A100 delivered far more throughput per watt for AI; for instance, 1×A100 could outperform multiple V100s in DL training, more than justifying the power increase.
Summary: Ampere A100 dramatically expanded AI capabilities with new tensor operations (TF32, BF16) and features like MIG and sparsity. It also continued to scale up memory and multi-GPU communication. These improvements made A100 the de facto choice for large-scale training in 2020–2022, offering unprecedented versatility – from single GPU partitions for inference to massive clusters for training – all with improved energy efficiency over the previous generation.
NVIDIA Hopper (H100, 2022) – Transformer Engine and FP8 Precision
Hopper pushed the envelope further, targeting the exploding complexity of AI models (especially Transformers) with new numeric formats and engine-level optimizations. The NVIDIA H100 (GH100 GPU) introduced 4th-gen Tensor Cores with FP8 and a dedicated Transformer Engine, alongside significant jumps in core count, memory speed, and interconnects. Key highlights of the H100 include:
- Advanced Process & Multi-Tile Design: Built on TSMC’s 4N (optimized 5nm) process, H100 grew to 80 billion transistors in a 814 mm² die, and certain configurations even used a multi-module approach (H100 NVL) for increased memory. It features 132 SMs with 16,896 FP32 cores, more than 2.4× the cores of A100. At ~1.55 GHz base (~1.8 GHz boost), H100 achieves on the order of 60–67 TFLOPS FP32 and 30+ TFLOPS FP64 (the first NVIDIA GPU to support FP64 at full rate via tensor cores). This is a huge jump in general compute throughput. TDP for data-center H100 (SXM5) ranges up to 500W, with special versions (H100 NVL for inference) configured at 700W.
- Fourth-Gen Tensor Cores & FP8: Hopper’s tensor cores added support for FP8 (8-bit floating point) number formats, on top of FP16/BF16/TF32. Each H100 tensor core can perform FP8 matrix ops at twice the rate of FP16. Leveraging this, the H100 delivers ~1,000 TFLOPS of tensor compute (FP16 or FP8 with sparsity) – over 3× A100’s throughput. For example, dense tensor performance is ~990 TFLOPS FP16 and ~1980 TOPS (tera-ops) INT8/FP8 on H100, versus 312/624 on A100. This enormous throughput is particularly beneficial for deep learning.
- Transformer Engine (TE): To effectively use FP8 for AI, NVIDIA introduced a Transformer Engine in H100 – a combination of software and hardware that dynamically chooses between FP8 and FP16 precision for different layers of Transformer models. The TE maintains model accuracy while maximizing speed. This innovation made H100 incredibly fast on NLP and generative models: up to 9× faster training and 30× faster inference on large language models compared to A100. In practical terms, an H100 can train giant Transformer networks (with FP8 precision) almost an order of magnitude quicker, and serve inference for massive models in real-time where A100 struggled.
- Confidential Computing: Hopper added engine-level security features. It’s the first GPU with memory encryption and isolated execution environments, enabling confidential computing where model weights and data can be processed with hardware-level encryption. This feature is vital for privacy in multi-tenant cloud deployments and was absent in prior generations.
- New DPX Instructions: H100 introduced DPX (dynamic programming accelerators) – specialized instructions to accelerate algorithms like sequence alignment and dynamic programming used in genomics, finance, and operations research. This broadened the scope of GPU acceleration beyond AI/HPC into these domains.
- Memory System: H100 comes with 80 GB of HBM3 (SXM5 model) at 3.35 TB/s bandwidth, a ~2.2× bandwidth increase over A100. It actually features 5 HBM3 stacks (each 16 GB) on the SXM, and a PCIe variant with HBM2e. Later, a 6-stack configuration (96 GB) was enabled for certain H100 models. The larger, faster memory allows training bigger models and holding more activations on-chip. L2 cache also grew substantially (50 MB) to keep the many SMs fed.
- NVLink 4 and High-Speed Networking: Hopper’s NVLink v4 links run at 900 GB/s per GPU (18 links × 50 GB/s). This high-bandwidth interconnect, combined with H100’s support for PCIe 5.0, meant faster multi-GPU training and IO. NVIDIA’s DGX H100 servers leverage NVLink and dual InfiniBand NICs to minimize communication bottlenecks – e.g., 8× H100 in DGX form a 32 PFLOPS AI system with 2× faster networking than the previous generation, improving scaling efficiency and energy efficiency for large clusters.
- Grace CPU and NVLink-C2C: Though not part of the H100 GPU silicon, 2022 also saw NVIDIA introduce the Grace CPU Superchip (two 72-core ARM Neoverse V2 CPUs) and NVLink-C2C (chip-to-chip) connectivity. In Grace-Hopper (GH100) Superchips, one Grace CPU is tightly coupled to one H100 GPU via a coherent NVLink-C2C interface (up to 900 GB/s). This allows the CPU and GPU to share memory and operate as a unit, benefiting CPU-intensive AI workloads. The GH100 (Grace+H100) configuration foreshadowed even tighter integration in the subsequent generation.
Summary: Hopper H100 accelerated the AI revolution with specialized features for Transformer models and 8-bit precision, while also pushing general-purpose compute and memory to new heights. It provided huge performance gains on AI (order-of-magnitude speedups on LLMs vs A100) albeit at higher power. Hopper also set the stage for CPU-GPU fusion (Grace-Hopper platform) to tackle data-hungry workloads with massive shared memory.
NVIDIA Hopper Refresh (H200 & Grace Hopper GH200, 2023–24) – Memory Boost and CPU–GPU Integration
As AI models continued to balloon in size (hundreds of billions of parameters), NVIDIA rolled out a mid-generation update: the Hopper H200 GPU and the Grace Hopper “GH200” Superchip, first unveiled in 2023. The focus was on memory – capacity, bandwidth, and CPU-GPU coherence – rather than raw compute increases. Key points in this evolutionary step:
- H100 to H200 – More Memory: The NVIDIA H200 is essentially an H100 with upgraded memory. It uses next-generation HBM3e memory technology, increasing GPU memory from 80 GB to 144 GB (officially ~141 GB usable) on a single GPU. This 1.8× increase in on-board memory enabled single GPUs to hold larger portions of models (important for giant LLMs). HBM3e also boosts bandwidth per stack, yielding roughly 5 TB/s memory bandwidth per H200 GPU (versus 3.35 TB/s on H100). The H200’s compute specs (SM count, clock, 80B transistors) remain based on Hopper architecture (same 16,896 CUDA cores and tensor cores), so raw TFLOPS are similar to H100. But the memory capacity and speed uplift helps memory-bound workloads and extremely large models.
- Grace Hopper GH200 Superchip: The GH200 pairs one Grace 72-core CPU with one H200 GPU in a single module, connected via NVLink-C2C at 900 GB/s. This is an evolution of the GH100 (Grace + H100) but with HBM3e. Crucially, the GH200 combines the CPU’s LPDDR5x memory (up to 480 GB at 0.5 TB/s) with the GPU’s 144 GB HBM3e, for a total of ~624 GB unified memory per module. The CPU and GPU can access each other’s memory coherently, effectively creating a huge pool for data or models. This design is extremely beneficial for massive recommender systems, graph analytics, and training or inference of trillion-parameter models that don’t fit in 80 GB or even 144 GB alone. Instead of spilling to slower host memory, everything stays on-package.
- H200 NVL and Multi-GPU Modules: NVIDIA also introduced an H200 NVL variant – a PCIe form-factor combining multiple H200 GPUs with NVLink – aimed at giant inference deployments. For example, an H200 NVL module with 4× H200 GPUs can provide 564 GB of HBM3e total and is interconnected with 1.8 TB/s GPU–GPU bandwidth (using an NVLink Switch). This allows those 4 GPUs to function with a shared memory space for inference on enormous LLMs. Essentially, H200 NVL is to inference what the earlier A100 NVLink (and H100 NVL) were: a solution to treat multiple physical GPUs as one logical GPU with more memory. It underscores that memory size, not just compute, was the bottleneck for cutting-edge AI models by 2024.
- Performance Impact: While H200 did not markedly increase compute throughput over H100, the effective performance on large models improved due to fewer memory swaps. NVIDIA indicated that connecting GH200 superchips in a 4-way module (the Quad GH200 or GH200 NVL4) significantly boosted certain workloads. For instance, a server with four GH200 (4× Grace + 4× H200) was used as a baseline to compare against the new Blackwell systems. The GH200 quad system already demonstrated the value of enormous combined memory and close CPU-GPU coupling, though in pure speed it was only a modest step above H100 in most cases (since compute didn’t change). The main gain was the ability to handle bigger problems more efficiently.
Summary: The H200 and Grace Hopper GH200 represented an evolutionary memory-centric upgrade – effectively bridging the gap to the next major architecture. By expanding memory and tying CPUs and GPUs closer together, NVIDIA enabled users to tackle models and datasets that previously required cumbersome multi-node solutions. This stage set the groundwork for the Blackwell generation, which would combine new architectures and the benefits of huge memory and CPU integration.
NVIDIA Blackwell (Grace Blackwell GB200, 2024–25) – Multi-Chip Module and 4-Bit AI Inference
Grace Blackwell GB200 is NVIDIA’s newest flagship in 2025, ushering in the Blackwell architecture for GPUs and fusing it with the Grace CPU in an even more powerful superchip. Blackwell-based GPUs bring sweeping architectural changes: a multi-die design to break the reticle limit, new low-precision modes for AI, and further improvements in memory and interconnect. The Grace-Blackwell GB200 platform, first announced at SC’23, represents the culmination of NVIDIA’s step-by-step evolution. Key innovations and differences in Blackwell (vs. the Hopper generation) include:
- Multi-Chip GPU Architecture: Blackwell is NVIDIA’s first flagship GPU to use a chiplet (MCM) design. The high-end configuration, often referred to as B200, actually consists of two GPU dies on one package connected by a 10 TB/s on-package link. Each die is at the reticle size limit (~≳ 100 billion transistors each), so together the B200 module packs an unprecedented 208 billion transistors. (For comparison, H100 was ~80B). This 2-die design allows ~2.6× more transistors than Hopper, enabling far more cores and cache. Yet, by using two slightly smaller dies instead of one monolith, NVIDIA can improve yield and binning for such a massive GPU. The chiplet interconnect is fast enough (10 TB/s) to make the two dies function as one “giant GPU.” From a software perspective, B200 is presented as a single GPU with unified memory and compute, despite being physically multi-die. This design increases manufacturing complexity but pays off in raw horsepower.
- Process Technology and Clock: Interestingly, NVIDIA built Blackwell on a refined TSMC 4N/4NP process, not a full node shrink to 3nm. The custom 4NP process likely offers speed and efficiency tweaks. Blackwell’s boost clocks are higher – roughly ~1.9–2.0 GHz – to drive its many cores. As a result, and combined with more cores, the theoretical compute of a B200 is massive: on the order of >130 TFLOPS FP32 and 40+ TFLOPS FP64 (exact spec not public, but clearly higher than H100’s 67/34 TFLOPS). Early data suggests an MCM B200 at 1000W can deliver about 2.3× the dense tensor throughput of H100 (e.g. ~2250 TFLOPS FP16 vs 990 on H100). This scaling is slightly less than linear (2×) due to overheads and power limits, but still a major leap. Blackwell continues to support FP64 tensor operations; however, the design emphasizes AI/matrix throughput even more than Hopper did, as seen by relatively modest FP64 gains but huge lower-precision gains. In short, Blackwell is built to do more work in parallel than any prior GPU, favoring AI and simulation workloads that can leverage its massive core counts.
- Second-Generation Transformer Engine with FP4: To advance AI capability, Blackwell GPUs introduce 4-bit floating point (FP4) support for inference. The Transformer Engine in Blackwell is upgraded to handle FP4 precision to further accelerate large language models and recommender inference. According to NVIDIA, this doubles the effective model size or compute capacity for AI inference, as models can be quantized to 4-bit with minimal accuracy loss for deployment. The new TE, combined with software (TensorRT-LLM, NeMo) algorithms for quantization and dynamic range management, lets Blackwell achieve higher throughput and handle models up to 10 trillion parameters in real-time inference. This is a major progression from Hopper’s FP8: Blackwell can trade a bit more precision for half the memory and bandwidth per parameter, which is critical at the extreme scales of generative AI models.
- Memory Capacity and Bandwidth: Each B200 GPU module contains 192 GB of HBM3e memory (comprised of 2×96 GB across the two dies). The memory is faster: running at 8 Gbps pin speed, the two-die package achieves 8.0 TB/s memory bandwidth – over 2× H100’s bandwidth. A single Grace-Blackwell GB200 superchip (which has 2× B200 GPUs + 1 Grace CPU) thus has 384 GB of HBM3e total, which the two GPU dies can access collectively. Additionally, the Grace CPU contributes up to 480 GB of LPDDR5X memory on the superchip. In a GB200, the CPU and GPU memories are unified via NVLink-C2C, giving a colossal ~864 GB pooled memory per module. This design enables training or inferencing of extreme-scale models fully in memory (the GPU HBM for high-speed scratch, and CPU memory for overflow) without resorting to host DDR or slow storage. The memory hierarchy is effectively tiered (HBM3e at 16 TB/s total for the superchip, CPU memory at ~0.5 TB/s), but all high bandwidth and coherent. Such memory-rich architecture is tailor-made for HPC and AI workflows dealing with terabyte-scale datasets or model states.
- Grace Blackwell Superchip and NVLink 5: The GB200 Grace-Blackwell Superchip connects 2× B200 GPU dies + 1× Grace CPU on the same module with 5th-gen NVLink-C2C links. This yields 900 GB/s CPU–GPU bandwidth like GH200, but now also GPU–GPU NVLink 5 on the module. NVLink 5 doubles per-GPU connectivity to 1.8 TB/s (bidirectional) – each Blackwell GPU has 18× NVLink5 links at 100 GB/s each, used to form large, fully connected clusters. In practical terms, NVLink5 allows up to 576 GPUs to be interconnected in a single accelerated cluster (e.g., 8 HGX B200 nodes of 8 GPUs each, fully meshed via NVLink Switches). The HGX B200 server platform uses 8× B200 GPUs connected with NVLink5 (no Grace CPU on that board) for x86 systems. Meanwhile, NVIDIA’s flagship deployment is the GB200 NVL72 – a rack-scale system combining 36 Grace-Blackwell superchips (72 GPUs + 36 CPUs) all linked via NVLink5 and NVSwitch fabric. The NVL72 forms a single giant 72-GPU topology with 130 TB/s of low-latency all-to-all bandwidth. This massive domain minimizes communication overhead for scaling AI training to many GPUs. The tight integration and fast interconnect also improve energy efficiency at scale, as less data needs to travel over slower, power-hungry links. NVIDIA claims a GB200 NVL72 delivers 30× faster LLM inference throughput than an equivalent H100 cluster, while reducing energy consumption by up to 25× for that workload. This highlights how Blackwell’s architecture is optimized for both performance and efficiency in large deployments.
- New Reliability and Data Engines: Given the scale at which Blackwell will operate (think: multi-exaflop AI supercomputers), NVIDIA equipped it with new engines for Reliability, Availability, Serviceability (RAS) and data loading. A dedicated RAS engine on each Blackwell GPU monitors and manages chip health, running AI-driven diagnostics to predict failures. This helps maximize uptime in clusters running 24/7 for weeks or months. There’s also a Data Decompression Engine in hardware, which speeds up loading of compressed datasets by offloading decompression to the GPU. As enterprise data pipelines often involve compressed data (e.g., for AI analytics or large databases), this can significantly boost I/O-bound workloads and free the CPU from handling decompression. These additions don’t directly increase “FLOPS,” but they remove bottlenecks and downtime, effectively raising sustained performance in production environments.
- Power Consumption and Efficiency: Each Blackwell B200 module is power-hungry – rated at ~1000W TDP for the SXM form factor. In a Grace-Blackwell superchip, with the CPU included, the module can draw up to ~1700–2700W under load (liquid cooling is employed in dense deployments). While this is ~1.4× the power per GPU of a Hopper, the performance gains are commensurate. In specialized tasks like LLM inference, Blackwell’s efficiency leap is striking: that 30× throughput vs H100 with 25× lower energy implies an order of magnitude better performance-per-watt. Even for more balanced HPC or training tasks, early benchmarks show substantial improvement per watt. For instance, a 4-GPU GB200 NVL4 (two Grace, four B200) outperforms a 4-GPU GH200 (Grace-Hopper) by ~2.2× in HPC simulations and ~1.8× in AI training, within a similar power envelope. This indicates Blackwell provides more work per joule despite the high absolute power – thanks to the efficient MCM design, higher perf/mm², and elimination of inter-node communication in many cases. Customers who cannot accommodate 1000W GPUs do have the option of a Blackwell B100 SKU (single-die, ~700W), which is pin-compatible with HGX H100 systems but with lower performance. This flexibility allows adoption of Blackwell’s efficiency improvements in existing datacenter infrastructure limits.
Summary: Grace Blackwell GB200 represents a major evolutionary leap, combining extreme GPU performance, massive memory, and tightly coupled CPU integration. Architectural innovations like multi-die GPUs, FP4 precision, and NVLink5 address the new bottlenecks of 2025: the need to train/infer ever-larger models quickly and efficiently. Compared to earlier generations, Blackwell is orders of magnitude ahead in certain AI capabilities (e.g. 4-bit inference, exascale LLM performance) while still advancing HPC throughput. Power has increased, but so has capacity and efficiency at scale – making the performance per watt significantly better for targeted workloads. The step-by-step improvements from Pascal’s HBM2 and NVLink1, through Volta’s tensor cores, Ampere’s sparsity and MIG, Hopper’s FP8 and Transformer Engine, to Blackwell’s FP4 and multi-chip design, illustrate NVIDIA’s iterative approach to pushing boundaries in compute, memory, and interconnect. Each generation built upon the last, removing the next bottleneck – be it precision, memory size, or data transfer – resulting in the Grace Blackwell superchips that can literally act as an AI supercomputer in a box.
Below, the key specifications of these GPU generations are compared side-by-side:
| GPU (Arch, Year) | Process | Transistors | FP64 TFLOPS (Tensor) | FP32 TFLOPS | Tensor AI Perf (FP16/FP8) | GPU Memory (Max) | Memory BW | NVLink (Bandwidth) | TDP (Board) | 
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tesla P100 (Pascal, 2016) | TSMC 16nm | 15.3B | 5.3 TFLOPS (no TC) | 10.6 TFLOPS | 21.2 TFLOPS FP16 (no TC) | 16 GB HBM2 | 0.72 TB/s | NVLink 1 (160 GB/s) | 300 W (SXM2) | 
| Tesla V100 (Volta, 2017) | TSMC 12nm | 21.1B | 7.8 TFLOPS (no TC) | 15.7 TFLOPS | ~125 TFLOPS FP16 (640 TCs) | 32 GB HBM2 | 0.90 TB/s | NVLink 2 (300 GB/s) | 300 W (SXM2) | 
| A100 (Ampere, 2020) | TSMC 7nm | 54.2B | 9.7 TFLOPS (19.5 TC) | 19.5 TFLOPS | 312 TFLOPS FP16 (624 w/ sparsity) | 80 GB HBM2e | 1.56–2.0 TB/s | NVLink 3 (600 GB/s) | 400 W (SXM4) | 
| H100 (Hopper, 2022) | TSMC 4N | 80.0B | 34 TFLOPS (67 TC) | ~60 TFLOPS | 990 TFLOPS FP16 (1980 FP8) | 80 GB HBM3 | 3.35 TB/s | NVLink 4 (900 GB/s) | 500 W (SXM5) 700 W NVL | 
| H200 (Hopper+, 2024) | TSMC 4N | 80.0B (same) | 34 TFLOPS (67 TC) | ~60 TFLOPS | 990 TFLOPS FP16 (1980 FP8) | 144 GB HBM3e | ~5.0 TB/s | NVLink 4 (900 GB/s) + NVLink-C2C 900 GB/s | 500 W (SXM5) | 
| Blackwell B200 (GB200, 2025) | TSMC 4NP | 208B (2×104B) | ~40 TFLOPS (FP64 TC) | ~130+ TFLOPS | 2250+ TFLOPS FP16 (4500+ FP8) | 192 GB HBM3e | 8.0 TB/s | NVLink 5 (1800 GB/s) + NVLink-C2C 900 GB/s | 1000 W (SXM6) | 
Generational Improvements at a Glance
- Architecture & Compute: Each generation saw new architectures (Pascal → Volta → Ampere → Hopper → Blackwell) bringing more cores and new compute units. Notably, Tensor Cores were introduced in Volta (2017) for FP16 matrix ops, refined in Ampere (TF32, BF16, sparsity) and Hopper (FP8 + Transformer Engine), and extended to FP4 in Blackwell for inference. General FP32/FP64 throughput also grew ~12× from Pascal to Blackwell.
- Memory Capacity: Grew from 16 GB (Pascal) to 80 GB (Hopper), then 144–192 GB with Hopper refresh and Blackwell. Integrated CPU memory in Grace superchips adds hundreds of GB more, letting latest GPUs address >0.5 TB of memory directly. This progression enables training/inference of ever-larger models in-memory.
- Memory Bandwidth: Increased roughly 10× over the decade – 0.72 TB/s (P100) to 8.0 TB/s (Blackwell). Each new HBM generation (HBM2 → HBM2e → HBM3 → HBM3e) and wider interfaces contributed to feeding the compute units with data. Hopper’s move to HBM3 and Blackwell’s to faster HBM3e were critical for keeping their massive core counts utilized.
- Interconnect & Scalability: NVLink bandwidth jumped each generation (160 GB/s → 300 → 600 → 900 → 1800 GB/s per GPU), and NVLink Switch architecture allows ever-larger GPU clusters (from 8-GPU systems with Pascal to >>500 GPUs with Blackwell). Additionally, NVLink-C2C in Grace superchips introduced CPU-GPU coherence at 900 GB/s, eliminating the PCIe bottleneck. These advancements drastically improve multi-GPU training efficiency and multi-node scaling.
- AI Capability: Each generation unlocked new AI workloads: Pascal had basic FP16; Volta brought 10× faster DL training with FP16 tensor cores; Ampere introduced sparsity and MIG for flexible inference; Hopper targeted Transformers with FP8 and delivered 30× faster inference than Ampere; Blackwell adds 4-bit inference and even larger model support, promising another big leap in throughput at lower precision. This trajectory has enabled the AI models of each era – from early CNNs on Pascal to today’s multi-trillion parameter models on Blackwell – to be trained and deployed at feasible speeds.
- Power Efficiency: Despite rising absolute power, performance per watt improved through architectural efficiency and specialization. Ampere A100 delivered more performance at 400W than V100 did at 300W (roughly 3× the throughput for 1.3× power in AI tasks). Hopper H100 at 500W delivered 9× the training speed of A100. Blackwell, though rated up to 1000W, excels in perf-per-watt for large deployments – e.g. 30× the inference performance of H100 for 1/25th the energy in a full rack. These efficiency gains come from better use of silicon (tensor cores, sparsity, etc.) and reduced data movement (more on-die memory, faster links), which amortize the power cost over much greater useful work.
Conclusion
Over the past generations, NVIDIA’s GPUs have transformed from general-purpose accelerators into highly specialized engines for AI and HPC. Each step – Pascal’s memory and NVLink, Volta’s tensor cores, Ampere’s scaling and sparsity, Hopper’s transformer optimizations, and Blackwell’s multi-die design with 4-bit precision – addressed the next bottleneck in performance, whether it was compute density, data throughput, or model size. In 2025, the Grace Blackwell GB200 stands as a convergence of these advancements: a superchip with CPU and GPU unified, massive fast memory, and an architecture tuned for the most demanding AI of our time. This step-by-step evolution showcases NVIDIA’s strategy of iterative innovation, delivering generational performance leaps while expanding capabilities and improving efficiency, which together have fueled the rapid progress in AI and scientific computing in recent years.
